terms.def: A distinct market state (bull/bear, high/low volatility, risk-on/off) with characteristic return distributions.
Hidden Markov Model — models regimes as latent states; observed returns are emitted from the current hidden regime.
Generalised Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity — models time-varying volatility; high vol clusters with high vol.
A date where the statistical properties of returns shift; detected by CUSUM, Bayesian Online CP Detection, etc.
Macro signal (yield curve, PMI, unemployment) used to identify economic regime; leads equity returns by 6–12 months.
Adjust factor/asset weights based on detected regime; e.g. favour quality/defensive in bear regimes.

eli3: Financial markets don't behave the same way all the time. In bull markets, momentum works well and value lags; in bear markets or recessions, quality and defensive factors outperform while momentum crashes. Regime detection is the process of identifying which market 'state' you're currently in so you can allocate to strategies that work in that regime.

A regime is a period where markets share similar statistical properties: high vs. low volatility, risk-on vs. risk-off, recession vs. expansion. Different regimes respond differently to the same signals. A strategy that earns 1% per month in a bull market might lose 2% per month in a bear market. Knowing the regime allows you to dial down strategies that typically fail in the current environment.

Regime detection uses statistical models (Hidden Markov Models, clustering, change-point detection) and macro indicators (yield curve, credit spreads, VIX) to classify the current market state. The goal is not prediction — it's identification of current conditions to inform near-term allocation.

whenWhy.title: Hidden Markov Models for market regimes
whenWhy.body: Hidden Markov Model (HMM): latent state S_t ∈ {1,2,...,K} (hidden regimes) generates observed returns r_t. Transition matrix P: P_ij = P(S_{t+1} = j | S_t = i) captures persistence of regimes. Emission distribution: r_t | S_t = k ~ N(μ_k, σ_k²). Estimation: Baum-Welch algorithm (EM for HMM) iterates: (E-step) compute posterior P(S_t = k | r_1,...,r_T) using forward-backward algorithm; (M-step) update μ_k, σ_k, and P. Filtered state probability at time T: P(S_T = k | r_1,...,r_T) — the probability of being in regime k given all available data. Two-state model: typically identifies a 'bull' regime (positive returns, low vol) and 'bear' regime (negative returns, high vol). Regime persistence: if P_{bull,bull} = 0.97, average duration of bull regime ≈ 1/(1-0.97) = 33 months.

whenWhy.title: Macro-based regime indicators
whenWhy.body: Instead of purely statistical regime models, use economic variables that predict regime transitions: (1) Yield curve slope (10y - 2y spread): inverted yield curve predicts recession 6-18 months later with ~70% accuracy (Campbell & Shiller); (2) Credit spread (HY - IG or IG - Treasury): widening spreads signal financial stress; HY spread > 5% historically precedes equity drawdowns; (3) VIX level: VIX > 25 = risk-off/stress regime; VIX < 15 = risk-on/complacent regime; (4) ISM Manufacturing PMI: above 50 = expansion; below 50 = contraction; (5) Employment: non-farm payrolls, unemployment rate trends; (6) Fed funds rate: tightening cycle vs. easing cycle has different factor return patterns. Macro regime indicator: composite score of the above variables, normalised and combined. Conditional factor performance: estimate factor returns separately for each macro regime; allocate dynamically to factors with highest expected return in current regime.

whenWhy.title: Clustering-based regime detection
whenWhy.body: Unsupervised approach: cluster historical market environments without pre-specifying regimes. Features: rolling 3-month equity return, 3-month bond return, 3-month VIX change, credit spread change. K-means clustering into K = 4-6 clusters. Label each cluster economically post-hoc: 'risk-on expansion,' 'risk-off contraction,' 'rate shock,' 'slow growth.' Map each historical month to its cluster; compute average factor returns within each cluster. Dynamic allocation: at each month, identify which cluster the current environment most resembles (using Euclidean distance to cluster centroids); tilt portfolio toward factors with historically high returns in that cluster. Validation: out-of-sample regime-conditional performance must be consistent with in-sample; if regime-tilting doesn't improve Sharpe out-of-sample, the clustering captures noise not genuine regime structure.

deepDiveIntro: Change-point detection identifies when the statistical properties of a time series have shifted. The CUSUM test accumulates deviations from a target mean and signals when the cumulative total exceeds a threshold. Bayesian online change-point detection maintains a running posterior probability over how long ago the last structural break occurred. PELT finds the optimal set of change points for an entire historical dataset by minimizing a penalized likelihood efficiently. The Chow test checks whether regression parameters differ significantly before and after a candidate break date. In portfolio management, detecting change points in factor return distributions triggers recalibration of the factor model.

deepDive.heading: Regime Detection in Practice: Challenges
deepDive.body: Regime detection faces fundamental difficulties. Regimes are only clear in hindsight: real-time filtered probabilities from an HMM are much noisier than in-sample classifications, and during the early weeks of the 2008 crisis the model may have been only 70% confident of a bear regime. Transitions can also be very fast — the 2020 COVID crash lasted 33 days, too quick for most monthly-frequency models to react meaningfully. Unsupervised cluster labels have no intrinsic economic meaning, and the models are prone to overfitting because they have many parameters relative to the number of observed regime transitions. Using macro indicators with clear economic interpretation alongside statistical models and limiting the model to two or three regimes are the main practical mitigations. [1]

deepDive.heading: Factor Timing with Regime Models
deepDive.body: Even accurate regime identification does not guarantee successful factor timing. Research by Asness et al. (2017) finds that no reliable timing signal outperforms equal-weight factor portfolios after transaction costs, with the best signals improving Sharpe by only about 0.1 to 0.2. The most robust timing signals are factor momentum — factors that performed well recently tend to continue over one to three months — and soft weighting of positions by regime probability rather than hard binary on-off switching, which reduces turnover and avoids whipsawing. [2]

deepDive.heading: Volatility Regimes and GARCH-Based Detection
deepDive.body: Volatility clustering provides a natural statistical basis for regime detection: high-volatility periods tend to follow high-volatility periods. A GARCH model captures this by making today's variance a weighted average of yesterday's conditional variance and yesterday's squared shock. Regime-switching GARCH allows the GARCH parameters themselves to differ across regimes. Practical uses include volatility targeting, where position sizes scale inversely with the current conditional volatility estimate, and regime-conditional VaR calculations that adapt to the current market environment. [1]

takeaways: HMM regime detection: latent states generate observed returns; Baum-Welch estimates μ_k, σ_k, transition matrix P. [1]
Macro indicators: yield curve inversion, credit spreads, VIX > 25 — more interpretable than statistical-only models. [1]
Factor timing research: regime-based timing improves Sharpe by only 0.1-0.2 after costs (Asness et al. 2017). [2]
Use regime probability as soft weight multiplier — not binary on/off — to reduce turnover. [2]
Volatility targeting (position ∝ 1/σ_t) is the most widely validated regime-based risk management approach. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you avoid over-fitting regime models to in-sample data?
edgeCases.a: Regime model overfitting is severe because the model can perfectly fit any return history by choosing enough regimes and flexible transition dynamics. Guards: (1) Restrict K (number of regimes): 2-3 is usually optimal; 4+ regimes rarely improve out-of-sample; (2) Information criteria: choose K that minimises BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion = -2 log L + K_params × log T); BIC penalises parameter count; (3) Out-of-sample test: split history 70/30; estimate model on 70%; evaluate regime-conditional factor returns on the 30%; (4) Parsimony principle: prefer economic regimes (recession vs. expansion using NBER dates) over statistical regimes — they have natural interpretation and can't be overfitted; (5) Block bootstrapping: bootstrap entire historical sequences (not random rows) to preserve regime persistence; test whether regime-conditional Sharpe improvements are robust to resampling.

edgeCases.q: What is the relationship between regime detection and crisis alpha?
edgeCases.a: Crisis alpha (term coined by Kathryn Kaminski): returns earned during market crises that are uncorrelated to, or negatively correlated with, equity risk. Trend following strategies naturally generate crisis alpha — they go short declining markets without any explicit regime detection. In contrast, regime detection-based factor timing aims to reduce allocation to strategies that fail in crises BEFORE the crisis is identified — which requires real-time accuracy. The empirical finding: systematic regime-based factor timing adds modest value; but allocating a dedicated sleeve to strategies with natural crisis alpha (trend following, long volatility, tail risk hedges) is more reliable than attempting regime-conditional tilts. The distinction: crisis alpha strategies are structurally defensive; regime-based timing is opportunistic. Both have a role in a multi-strategy portfolio, but with different implementation complexity and reliability.

sources.label: Hamilton, J.D. (1989) — A New Approach to the Economic Analysis of Nonstationary Time Series, Econometrica
Asness, C. et al. (2017) — Factor Momentum Everywhere, JPM
